Fear and Greed is at
34
(Fear)
and Bitcoin is at
$64K
this morning. Bitcoin held its ground over the weekend, trading
in a tight range between $63.5K and $64.5K as volume stayed thin
ahead of a pivotal week. The Fed's rate decision on July 29 is
now the market's focal point.

Positioning data tells the real story this Monday. Large traders
are making a specific, time-bound bet on where BTC goes next.
Meanwhile FTX creditors are days away from another major payout,
and stablecoin regulation just missed its own deadline.

Let's dive in.

Latest News
-----------

FTX Is Paying Out Billions to Creditors Again This Month

The FTX Recovery Trust will distribute approximately $900
million to creditors on July 31, its fifth payout since the
exchange's November 2022 collapse.

Total distributions will reach roughly $10 billion once this
round completes. Payments will go out through BitGo, Kraken, or
Payoneer within one to three business days.

What was the record date creditors needed to meet to be
eligible?

( https://link.coinmarketcap.com/f/a/JBXqLJFcFTmcBlQPDjqXTg~~/AAQRxRA~/nldvwdh61IwcnhHVhlo_sX11eTXxdmwoqOJaf5NvA7iBxL8EoMw-Il3U6xJJrwc8hfLdKhZtIWHRwzuCBnKaDjMox-DXfOnpEfVlmb5N8CSdvrr3zudVhoLUDIUNJXRNjEmkb9Qf00vJE7qyARbiikYcJWOftAonuoHxf3MI7aY~ )

76216.subject: The Iran war is America’s least popular since polls began
76216.body:
The Economist’s week ahead

Автор: The Economist


Автор: The Economist

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/iran-war-americas-least-popular-since-polls-began-the-economist-uc8je?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3Bb9ashcwDRBu21y5n7G1ESg%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=1_yOS0dbZlRsk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mrt3g22a~wn-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mrt3g22a-wn
  
Enjoying this newsletter? Get it in your inbox every weekend, as well as our daily round-up of The Economist’s best journalism, by signing up for free here. The war in Vietnam took six years to become as hated by Americans as the Iran war has become in six months. Net approval for Donald Trump’s war among Democratic voters is a solidly abject -84%. But here’s a surprise: for the first time, many more than half of his supporters approve only “somewhat” of how he’s handling his job, according to a Washington Post/Ipsos poll. Elsewhere: China’s young billionaires have made their wealth from video games, tea, AI and Labubus. This generation of tycoons is China’s most global—and its most enigmatic. Stephanie Studer, Senior digital editor What to expect in the week ahead: ▸ Andy Burnham is sworn in as Britain’s new prime minister today. He is expected to announce his cabinet. Investors have been nervously waiting for his pick for chancellor. Shabana Mahmood, the current home secretary, has reportedly emerged as a frontrunner. ▸ Joseph Aoun, Lebanon’s president, will meet Donald Trump for the first time on Tuesday. It has been nearly 20 years since a Lebanese head of state has visited the White House. Mr Aoun will reportedly present a plan on how to disarm Hizbullah, a militant group in Lebanon, and secure Israel’s withdrawal from the country. ▸ Alphabet reports its second-quarter earnings on Wednesday. The company’s market value rose by $2trn over 12 months, and in June it issued an $85bn share offering, the biggest-ever corporate equity-raising exercise. But its results come amid investor concerns about AI spending. Along with other tech giants, Alphabet’s stock fell in June. Editor’s picks The Iran war is America’s least popular since polls began Not far from Martin Luther King’s grey stone memorial in Washington is a big golden cup honouring Donald Trump. The “Iran War Participation Trophy”, erected by anonymous satirists, suggests that on the battlefield it is not the winning that counts but the taking part. “While some concern themselves with…measurable outcomes, President Trump demonstrated the courage to participate,” reads the inscription. China’s mysterious new billionaires are conquering the world Early last year, when DeepSeek, a Chinese artificial-intelligence lab, was causing panic among its Western rivals, almo

76215.subject: How to Build an AI Monopoly: The New Rules of Startup Strategy 🦄
76215.body:
View this post on the web at https://linas.substack.com/p/how-to-build-an-ai-monopoly

👋 Hey, Linas here! Every day, I break down 3 stories shaping the future of FinTech & Artificial Intelligence - plus the money movements and trends worth tracking. First time here? 397k+ FinTech and AI leaders get this daily. Join them:
We are living through the greatest value creation event in the history of technology - and most of the people building it will capture none of the value.
And that is not pessimism. It is pure arithmetic. U.S. private AI investment hit $285.9 billion in 2025, more than 23 times China’s, with 1,953 newly funded AI startups in the United States alone, and enterprise spending on generative AI more than tripled, from $11.5 billion to $37 billion (Stanford HAI AI Index 2026, Menlo Ventures). At the same time, the cost of intelligence is collapsing. Anthropic’s Opus-class flagship [ https://substack.com/redirect/96177bc0-14d5-4253-8762-cf44c75a2e92?j=eyJ1IjoiMXN4Zm00In0.wehL2Vke0PUDz1PHD6ZKtcy1ufIoeqpblecCEEWPpqI ] now sells for a third of its 2025 price, $5 per million input tokens against $15 in the Opus 4.1 era, the cost of matching any fixed level of model performance is falling by up to two orders of magnitude a year, and open-weight models like GLM-5.2 [ https://substack.com/redirect/f89e2c3e-16de-440d-baed-1498e93cef9b?j=eyJ1IjoiMXN4Zm00In0.wehL2Vke0PUDz1PHD6ZKtcy1ufIoeqpblecCEEWPpqI ] trail the closed frontier by roughly just four months.
In other words, anyone can now build an impressive AI product in a weekend. Which means an impressive AI product is worth almost nothing.
Here is the defining trap of the AI age: the technology massively increases the value being created while competition relentlessly drives the value captured toward zero. Thousands of founders are about to learn, expensively, that a great demo and a fine-tuned model in a crowded category add up to a company worth approximately nothing.
But there is a framework for escaping this trap. It was laid out a decade ago in a 40-minute Stanford lecture that has quietly become the most important strategy text of the AI era, and almost everyone building in AI today is violating its every principle.
What follows moves in three steps, each building on the last:
The framework itself: the value-capture formula, the two kinds of businesses, the lies companies tell about their markets, and the psychology that pulls smart founders into unwinnable fights.
The translation to AI in 2026: commoditizing models, thin-wrapper economics, agents, vertical AI, and the new moats of context, memory, and evals.
The tools for Monday morning: twelve concrete plays, a monopoly scorecard, a “kill the idea” checklist, seven questions every AI founder must answer, and a 90-day operating plan.
Bonus: the entire playbook is codified as an installable Claude Skill, built to work alongside the twelve-skill startup AI OS [ https://substack.com/redirect/edb4d567-09b2-41a0-8da6-fdd72f9a5438?

76212.subject: Agentic Payments on Avalanche: The Layer After Trust
76212.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.team1.blog/p/agentic-payments-on-avalanche-the

Identity tells an agent who a counterparty is. Reputation tells it whether to trust them. But the event that actually closes the loop is payment, and once agents pay each other hundreds of times an hour, the chain underneath stops being a detail.
Months ago I pointed an autonomous browser agent at Snowtrace.io to take a tedious data-collection job off my hands. It worked through the pages with a strange patience, clicking buttons and reading tooltips, slow but steady, until it froze on a dropdown menu and failed the task one step from the finish line.
So I asked our front-end team at Routescan for a small, non-invasive change to the interface the agent kept tripping over. They shipped the fix the next day. I reran the task, the agent cleared the dropdown, and it handed me the files I needed. A few days later it hit me what we had actually done: that commit was the first change we had ever shipped on Snowtrace for a machine instead of a human. I had spent months with our UX team on microcopy and user journeys, and almost without noticing we wrote our first iteration for machine experience instead of user experience.
That is a small story about a dropdown. It is also the leading edge of a larger reordering, and the part that gets the least attention is money.
Want to keep up with the latest in the Avalanche ecosystem? Subscribe to the Team1 blog and get our updates in your inbox weekly.
The Piece Everyone Skips
Two recent Team1 blog posts map this territory well.
Chris Fusillo of XKOVA framed ERC-8004 as a trust layer - focusing on identity, reputation, and validation to answer whether to trust an agent.
Meanwhile, Giacomo Barbieri (Jack) made the concept concrete by deploying an identity for his agent, Eva, on the C-Chain and pricing tasks in AVAX, answering who an agent is and what its track record says.
Identity and reputation are the discovery half of the problem. They tell an agent which counterparty to pick. But picking is not transacting. Between “I trust this agent” and “the work is done and paid for” sits a settlement event, and that event is the one that has quietly broken the open web for thirty years.
HTTP has had a status code for it since 1997. 402 Payment Required has sat unused the entire time, because there was never a settlement layer cheap and fast enough to invoke it. Card networks have fixed fees that make sub-dollar payments absurd. PayPal and its cousins demand accounts and KYC. First-generation blockchains had fees too high and throughput too low to settle anything granular. So the web aggregated instead: monthly subscriptions, bundles, paywalls. We pay for the month because nobody could bill us for the minute. That is a workaround for a settlement problem, not a design anyone loved.
Agents do not tolerate the workaround, because they do not transact like us.
Why Avalanche Fits Agentic Payments
1. The fee math only works a

76211.subject: OpenAI forces Anthropic to keep Fable
76211.body:
----------
######                  [Collaborate with us](https://utm.genai.works/r/he2t9cz)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** OpenAI's new model is so good it forced Anthropic to officially keep Fable in subscriptions after all, a Chinese open model matched Fable and sold out in two days, an autonomous agent broke into Hugging Face over a weekend and Google wiped $200 billion off its own value delaying Gemini.



**In today's Generative AI Newsletter:**

* **Anthropic:** Why can't Anthropic take Fable away anymore?

* **Moonshot:** What model sold out in 48 hours?

* **Hugging Face:** How did one agent breach a whole company?

* **Google:** What cost Google $200 billion in a day?


----------



----------
## [OpenAI forced Anthropic to keep Fable](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15424964-claude-fable-5-on-your-plan)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/06d2a4dd-db17-4ff5-9e6b-7f3efde318fd/Beehiiv_01_-_Jul_20.png?t=1784541504)
Caption: 

Anthropic made its call on Fable 5 but it's permanent this time. Starting today, Max and Team Premium keep the model at half their usual limits. Pro and Team Standard lose it, with a one-time $100 credit as a parting gift.

That $100 goes fast. At Fable's rates a single heavy session eats the whole thing, so for Pro users the real message is upgrade or drop to a weaker model.

The plan a week ago was to cut Fable from subscriptions entirely and charge by the token. GPT-5.6 Sol killed that idea.

With OpenAI matching Fable on the benchmarks at a third the cost, pulling your best model from the $200 plan hands every wavering subscriber a reason to leave.

So the best model stays, but only for the people paying the most.

We said the goodbye to Fable would be the next hello to Opus 5. Anthropic found a third door. No Opus 5, Fable stays, and the Pro tier keeps it.


----------



----------
## [The AI company Nvidia and Google pay to reach new buyers](https://utm.genai.works/r/lirgco2)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/d9797fc3-421b-4f85-b3c3-8915655fcbb9/ChatGPT_Image_Jul_20__2026__12_58_41_PM.png?t=1784541576)
Caption: 

Anyone can build an AI product now. Getting it in front of real buyers is the hard part, and that's what GenAI Works does.

ToneUp turns data from a 14M+ community into distribution no competitor can reach, which is why 300+ enterprise customers already trust us.

The numbers behind it:

* 2.5x revenue growth last year.

* Backed by 300+ enterprise customers, including Nvidia, Oracle, Google and IBM.
